speaker: interviewer
text: '95. We're in Sydney, Australia. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you recovered again, and then you were peeling potatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Russians, yes, because they supplied, you know, food for the hospital. And I remember the cook there, he cooked something with condensed milk, those tins of condensed milk. And half of them they left in. And we thought, oh, you know, that's a lot in. We'll take them. But we were scared. But, but after all, they throw it out. They wouldn't do anything. But we thought we were stealing. We were so scared [INAUDIBLE] because what we went through with the Germans. And we took a whole lot of the tins, you know, we took them home. And there was a little boy before he went to the hospital, and we spooned it out. There was just a lot of the thick milk in. It was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you think that you survived that when other people died from eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. For a purpose, I suppose. I've got six children. Must be, because my husband survived. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened then, after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. Then the Americans came and put us in trucks to go to Leipzig to the American zone. And they took me straight away to sit in the front, you know. I was a young girl. I got chocolates. [LAUGHS] It was lovely. We stayed a couple of days in Leipzig. And then trucks came again and took us to the slums of Holland, not Amsterdam straight away. We stayed there a few days, and you know, we got, you know, plenty to eat. And Friday night we were invited to some people, because the south of Holland was liberated long before the north. So there were kind of settled already. And I never forget those people, you know. They gave us a lovely night. And they said now we've got friends in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll give you the address, and when you-- I'm ashamed to say that-- and when you're in Amsterdam, you'll give them the parcel with cheese in it. Do you think the cheese came to those people? We ate it. [LAUGHS] Fancy doing that after people coming from the camp. Yeah. That's what happened, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after a few days, trucks came again and we went to Amsterdam. And we went to the central station. It was the receiving center. And where the registration, you know, different departments-- police, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] distribution, anything, you know-- all what you need for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] registration. And there was the Jewish authorities. When I said my name, they said, you're the fiancee of Nick Melkman. And he's here too. He comes every day to see you. He saw you on the list. Just now he's not here. They gave me his address.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was put in a-- what used to be an orphanage-- and as soon as I came there, I said, can I use your phone? And they thought I was mad because, you know, who uses the phone straight after the war? They had to be very economical. When I told them why, I could. And I rang the home where he was, and he wasn't there. I left a message. And I went upstairs in the ward with about five people. And well, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, you know, we all had the runs, you know, we had to go to toilet. And I was just trying to find clean clothes for myself. And there was a knock at the door. And it was one of the servants and said, he is there. He's downstairs. So I had no undies on. [LAUGHS] And I went downstairs, running downstairs, the last steps I couldn't, you know, and he was waiting downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like to see him again after all that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what's that? And he had a, a scar here. It was one of the kapos. He said I was in the camp, in the first five minutes, I was in his way. And he copped it. And I said, your ring? And he said, I lost it. I said, so did I. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How had you lost your ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in Trebitz, I kept it all the time. But I lost so much weight that one morning I went to the toilet and I dropped it in. It fell off my finger. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other things that you kept during the whole time that you were in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Some papers. Yeah, some papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of papers could you keep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, like Betty's birth certificate, and things like that, you know. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had any contact with your husband before this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't your husband at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No news from him at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that night we went straight away to see those people, you know, who got these letters. We didn't even talk about that, did we? You don't know about that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we should-- we should go back and talk about that. Yes. OK, if you could tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they had some clothes for us. My mother gave, you know, at the time, if anyone comes back, here's some underwear. If they're badly needed, it was there. And yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. If you could tell me about the letters that you got from your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my husband, he went to a camp, you know, after he was picked up in the street. And he went to the prison and travelled with my parents. And he came in a camp, Seibersdorf, which was not quite finished. And it was a railway going. And he saw that had coal trains going to Trieste in Italy. And he thought, if I can get on a train like that, you know? And at the same time, there was somebody that said, hey Nick, what about escaping? And Nick said, tonight, straight away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somehow they climbed the fence and they came to the railway line. And I said it was arranged. The train came, and they climbed in the brake compartment, and they travelled and travelled until they came near-- I can't remember where. But the train stopped, and they were caught. And they said, well, we had a bombardment in Stettin, and our names are so-and-so-- different names. We're scared for the bombs, and we went to get away from it. So they took them to Munich and courthouse, and they were sent to the-- how they call it here? The work, S, to get work, the Social Security. Alright.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Social Security. And he got a job at a butcher, and the other boy got another job somewhere else. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We might have to skip the rest of his story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. But while he was free, he sent a letter to friends of mine, and I was still at home and I got that letter. And we sent him clothes, be he never got it, because they had to go through it again. Anyhow, to make things short, he was caught again at the Swiss border and sent to prison, to Innsbruck, where he stayed a year. He put on weight there. He had a good time there working in library. And then they couldn't find out who he was. Then investigated in Holland those people who got the letter. And they said, do you know Hank [? Fissel? ?] And the man said, yes, I know Hank [? Fissel. ?] I used to work with him on the docks. And he's a tall fellow with grey hair. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the false name that your husband had had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was he writing to them at the time that he was in that prison in Innsbruck as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's why they went to the address of that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things would he say in those letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, hidden. Was is all right, because he was in prison. I'm all right here. There's some people who try to get to the Swiss front here, but I wouldn't do a thing like that. You know, like that. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it all sort of secretly worded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, like guarded. Guarded, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these friends would pass the letters on to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, when I was home. But later on they had to keep 'em.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you were reunited, you went to visit these friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had underwear that my mother's gave. [INAUDIBLE] Before my mother went, she said, you take this and take that. If anyone comes back, you know, give them. At least I've got something to wear. Which was, you know, Godsent. Godsent to have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things did you have there that they gave back to you? It was clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underwear mainly, mainly, and all the photos. Lots of photos. And a few things, but they kept. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Then there was not much for us to do in Holland. We did-- my husband used to be in cattle transport. And when he saw big trucks with the names on it, and there used to be anti-Jewish, they threw him out of a job. And they had the big jobs now. He didn't feel like staying there. And in the meantime, there were posters everywhere. Join the Marines, you take your training in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he joined the Marines. And then when the war with Japan was finished, he got the training in-- in Holland to be sent to Indonesia, to Dutch East Indies, it was. I went to England. Some friends [? wanted ?] me there. When did you go to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In about November of that year, '45. He went into training, I went to England. And then in February, '46, he came over and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can I just ask you about that time in England without him? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good. You know, I was a good, you know, girl, you know, a young girl from the camps, you know. I had a fantastic time. I was-- you know, they had two daughters where I was. And I'm still friendly with them. They're like my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you do there in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just-- even the daughters didn't do anything to help the mother in the house. And they clothed me. And, you know, they gave me the wedding-- 300 people at the wedding, I didn't know anyone. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the wedding like for you after? Was it a happy time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was happy and it was sad, you know. No parents there. Nobody I knew. But they gave me a fantastic wedding. Then he had two weeks leave, then he left. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you miss him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then he came back after, after two weeks, I think, on embarkation leave. [INAUDIBLE] So we had another two weeks together. And then we said goodbye. And one morning, early in the morning was a telegram. Come to Southampton, because there were boat docks there. So quickly borrowed some money, got dressed, went to the station and to Southampton. About seven hours in the train. Found a boat at night in the dark. And we had another couple of days in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been very romantic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that was it. Then he went to Indonesia, and about a year later I followed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do over that year you were in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just, you know, helped the girls, went shopping with the girls. They did the shopping for the mother. And [SIGHS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- if we could just go back a little to back in Holland, you found out about your family at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the Red Cross, that [INAUDIBLE] they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your brother as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or just your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmothers, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that time must have been both--

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy and not happy. If I wouldn't have found Nick back, maybe I would have felt the loss of the parents more. But I was, it overruled, kind of, the happiness to find him back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't affected that much by it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because it was one good thing, and that was it. After all, that was future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when could you allow yourself to start really feeling happy and enjoying life again? When did that start to happen? Was that in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Indonesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when did you go to Indonesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, December '46. I was married in February, December, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So could you tell me about your time in Indonesia? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was lovely. Lovely at went there, but then my husband got sick. He got pneumonia and emphysema, and he was seven months in the hospital there. And I had two children then by that time. And he was put on a machine that pumped of puss, you know, that's in his [? lungs. ?] That's not water on, on the lungs, but puss. And bottles and bottles of puss. Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: An infection on the lungs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that from his wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yes. Then he started getting fits. He had fits, too, because of that, that cut he had. So he was not a healthy man after the war. Anyhow, he was dismissed from hospital and dismissed from, from the Marines, and sent back to Holland. There was a little bit of the stuff back in-- left in this lungs. They said it would disappear on its own. And-- and when we came in Holland, it started again. We didn't have a house to go to, but the Marines put us in a hotel from the Salvation Army. And there he got sick again. And he spent another eight months in hospital, got a lung operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like for you all these times he was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible, because in the meantime, we got a house allocated to us. First of all, we refused it, because it was in an attic, a refurbished attic, which we refused. Then we got a house allocated. He was in hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to clean it. I had to buy anything, from a teaspoon to curtains to bed, everything. So what did I do? I put the children in a private nursery. So I had my hands free and could do whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally I had it all going. The house was all ready, warmed up, I had a playpen for the youngest, everything. I can't pick up the children. I can't get my daughter because she had a mid-ear infection. I had to wait a few days. Got her, finally. Then I travel to the hospital all the time, because it was out of Amsterdam. Finally he's improved, he can come home. The children had whooping cough, he can't come home. [LAUGHS] But all ends well. All's well, ends well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he finally get to come home?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] '50, I think, in 1950. And then, of course, he didn't have a job. He didn't like Holland anymore. And we decided, his brother was here already, we decided to go to Australia. Don't know what to expect from Australia. Never a word of Australia. His brother was here, we go. [LAUGHS] Didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try and find out about it at all before you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because we were together. We had a family and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, and then got four more here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So can you tell me when you came out to Australia, and what was that like, the journey out to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was terrible, too, because we paid for our own passage. Not my husband, because he was an ex-militaire. The agency, the travel agency promised us-- it was a new boat, the maiden voyage-- you go to Genoa on the train, and there will be sleepers. And everything was beautiful. There were no sleepers, nothing. We came on the boat--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oceania. It was a brand new Italian boat. In Sicily, we picked up Italians, migrants. There were two sittings at the table. The first one spilled all the wine over the table, the second one would sit there. People-- women were feeding their babies at the table. Uh, we had such a terrible trip. Nobody to look after the children. And we thought it would be beautiful on a maiden voyage.

speaker: interviewee
text: But never mind. We came in Melbourne. [LAUGHS] There was a telegram--you wouldn't believe that-- a telegram from his brother. Please bring butter, as much as you can. Because there was a shortage of butter. And we couldn't understand. If there's no butter in Sydney, why should we bring it from Melbourne? [LAUGHS] We couldn't understand that. So we bought a whole box of butter. Because his father-in-law had a shop, and we bought a whole box of butter. [LAUGHS] His father-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, he got married in the meantime, because he was a Marine, too. And he had correspondence with that girl, and they sponsored him. And as soon as he came to Australia, he married that girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your impression of Australia when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went straightaway to my brother-in-law's house, because we brought our own home with us to be built here, prefabricated, but it hadn't arrived yet. So for the time being, we were with the in-laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he already bought a block of land in Loftus, in the southern interior, because that was an area where you were allowed prefabricated houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you arrive, and what was the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed with your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My husband found, straightaway, work in a store of a big-- it doesn't exist anymore, [INAUDIBLE], used to be a big grocery chain. And from then on, he had so many jobs. But his last job was for about 20 years, he worked in a Sutherland hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you briefly tell me about the different jobs that he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he worked for Parke-Davis. See, first of all, he worked in the city. And then in the Sutherland Shire they started to open up, you know, different opportunities. He worked for Parke-Davis, for Nemco, which was furniture. Pick and Shuffle, he did. Anything he could lay his hands on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was his health and your health over this time in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: His health? Well, no good, because he had those fits. Small ones, in the beginning. I noticed that in Indonesia, in his sleep, he used to do, like tics. And I thought he was joking, you know. But I couldn't wake him up from then on, you know. But at the end it wasn't that bad. He only had little attacks and had it under control.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your health? How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many children did you end up having?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got Herman. He'll be here later. He's the oldest. Sarah, that's my daughter. She might be here. Then I had David and Lester and Louis, he'll be here. Louis, and Jacqueline.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you sum up your years in Australia since you've arrived here? How would you say Australia has treated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Australia really itself hasn't done much, but being here-- I need them, let me put it that way. I didn't need Australia to do things for me. But being here and being free and having the children, it's a good country. Let me put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, so your health now, how is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the last year I start to feel my age a little bit, but it's not nothing serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that it's because of things that happened to you during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, because I'm 75. And you know, little, little, little complaints, not serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about with the dirty needles and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've had one operation on the, on the buttock. I had a cyst there. And the doctors reckon it was a dirty needle. I said, well, that's possible, because when I had the typhoid, I had plenty of needles. Yes, I had the operation '50, in about '55, 1956. It was a cyst on the buttock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying before that when you were here in Australia, it was-- it must've been quite hard with the six children.

speaker: interviewee
text: What struck me was the old cars you saw. And I had beautiful clothes with me for the children. You didn't see any nice clothes here. They were very far behind, I reckon, with everything. I, I remember on, near Museum Station, you know, they had the police in a high, like in a-- like a house on stilts, that regulating the traffic. There were no traffic lights. That's the first thing we noticed. And we've come a long way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you miss Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'll tell you, they still don't think much of Australia overseas. Because I had a letter from a cousin the other day, and she said-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I hear you're-- there's someone there-- I hear you're working in a museum. How come in Australia they have-- they think of the Holocaust? How come they have a Holocaust Museum in Australia? They have no idea. Right. So when you first came here, did you miss Holland and everything that was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you must have been quite busy over the time, with six children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had them spread out very well, because I had the first two just a year apart. And then after four years, I had two with 18 months space. And then another four years with 18 months space. Not, it wasn't broken up like that, but it just happened. Which was good, because I never had more than two at home. Then went to school. And it was lovely for them to grow up where we lived in the Sutherland Shire. It was very quiet. No traffic in the street. They could go all day, playing outside. My husband was a big help when he was home. He would always help out. When baby was born, he saw to it that he had his holidays. He was a good supplier and a good help. And I still miss him after 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So over this time, how much did you think about the war at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we think about it, yeah. We still think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it something which you find comes back to you more now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. If I wake up in the morning at 6 o'clock, I can't sleep anymore, thinking about everything, memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see people who are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that difficult for you to have these memories coming back?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can you do? What can you do? There's nothing you can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you say the lasting effects of the war for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me? Lost years. Really, lost years. I-- you probably have contact with girlfriends, school friends, you might have a school reunion. I can't talk with no one about my early days. There's no one there, no friends, no school friends, nobody. It's, it's a real miss. Even from when I was a teenager and went to work, all those friends, they're all gone, too. So that's an, an aftereffect very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you feel the loss that your children must have in not having grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's terrible too, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what message would you have, if you look back over the war years, is there anything that you've learnt from your experiences there? Anything that you'd like to tell the people watching this video, that you learned from that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hate the Germans, hate them. Don't trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that something you feel about all Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup. Yup. Because people say, yeah, but it's a new generation, they're different. That generation, God knows what they would have done if they would have won the war. And our generation, same generation isn't there anymore. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't forgive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not forgive and not forget. No, can't. Wouldn't you if they murdered your parents? Somebody murdered your parents? What would you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to say about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope I've, you know, helped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of Tape 4 of the interview with Charlotte Melkman. It's the 24th in March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
